item.name: ld
item.syntax: ld op1,op2
item.description: The ld instruction copies the value of the second operand into the first operand. It does not alter any of the flags, except for the special cases of reading the I or R registers. The two operands must fit in size; they can be either 8 or 16 bits long. The two tables below show all the possibilities. The first operand is on the left column, the second on the top row. Imm denotes an immediate (either 8 or 16-bit constant), n is a signed 8-bit integer (ranging from -128 to 127). Ofs is a 16-bit memory address.

note.description:
8-bit combinations
 immABCDEHLIXHIXLIYHIYL(BC)(DE)(HL)(IX+n)(IY+n)(ofs)IR
AXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX**
BXXXXXXXXXXXX  XXX   
CXXXXXXXXXXXX  XXX   
DXXXXXXXXXXXX  XXX   
EXXXXXXXXXXXX  XXX   
HXXXXXXXX      XXX   
LXXXXXXXX      XXX   
IXHXXXXXX  XX          
IXLXXXXXX  XX          
IYHXXXXXX    XX        
IYLXXXXXX    XX        
(BC) X                  
(DE) X                  
(HL)XXXXXXXX      **     
(IX+n)XXXXXXXX       **    
(IY+n)XXXXXXXX        **   
(ofs) X                  
I X                  
R X                  


* Alters the flags in the following way: preserves C; resets H and N; alters Z, S and P/V.
** It is possible to create these intructions manually by knowing the opcodes of the Z80 CPU, but these combinations give the halt instruction.

16-bit combinations
 immBCDEHLIXIYSP(ofs)
BCX      X
DEX      X
HLX      X
IXX      X
IYX      X
SPX  XXX X
(ofs) XXXXXX

item.name: push
item.syntax: push reg16
item.comments: reg16: AF, BC, DE, HL, IX, IY
item.description: When the instruction is executed, SP is decreased by two and the value of reg16 is copied to the memory location pointed by the new value of SP. It does not affect the flags.

note.description: Before using push you have to be sure that SP points to a safe place. You must also be aware that the call-ret instructions also use the very same stack to preserve the return address.

item.name: pop
item.syntax: pop reg16
item.comments: reg16: AF, BC, DE, HL, IX, IY
item.description: When the instruction is executed, the value of the word found at the memory location pointed by SP is copied into reg16, then SP is increased by 2. No flags are affected (except for the case of popping into AF).

note.description: You must be aware that the call-ret instructions also use the very same stack to preserve the return address.

group.name: Block operations
group.comments: Although these instructions are basically simple loads, they do affect the flags. S, Z and C are not altered, but H and N are set to zero. The P/V flag is interpreted as overflow. None of them have any operands. It is worth noting that a bunch of LDIs/LDDs are always faster than the corresponding LDIR/LDDR, so if you are optimising for speed, do not use the repeated load instructions.

item.name: ldi
item.description: The instruction copies a byte from (HL) to (DE) (i.e. it does an ld (de),(hl)), then increases both HL and DE to advance to the next byte. Besides, it decreases BC, and sets the P/V flag in the case of overflowing.

item.name: ldd
item.description: The instruction copies a byte from (HL) to (DE) (i.e. it does an ld (de),(hl)), then decreases both HL and DE to return to the previous byte. Besides, it decreases BC, and sets the P/V flag in the case of overflowing.

item.name: ldir
item.description: This is an ldi repeated until BC reaches zero. Naturally, the P/V flag holds zero after leaving the instruction, since BC does not overflow. After all, this single instruction copies BC bytes from (HL) to (DE), increases both HL and DE by BC, and sets BC to zero.

item.name: lddr
item.description: This is an ldd repeated until BC reaches zero. Naturally, the P/V flag holds zero after leaving the instruction, since BC does not overflow.

group.name: Exchange operations
group.comments: The values of the two operands are exchanged. There are a total number of five combinations possible: ex(sp),hl, ex(sp),ix, ex(sp),iy, exde,hl, exaf,af’. The last one naturally alters the flags (exchanges them with the shadow flags). You cannot exchange the order given, so there is no exhl,de!

item.name: ex
item.syntax: ex op1,op2
item.description: The values of the two operands are exchanged. There is a total number of five combinations possible: ex(sp),hl, ex(sp),ix, ex(sp),iy, exde,hl, exaf,af’. The last one naturally alters the flags (exchanges them with the shadow flags). You cannot exchange the order given, so there is no ex hl,de!

item.name: exx
item.description: There are no operands. This instruction exchanges BC with BC’, DE with DE’ and HL with HL’ at the same time. What’s very important to note that it is very fast.

item.name: add
item.syntax: add a,op8 (8 bits) or add reg16,reg16 (16 bits)
item.description: The values of the two operands are added together, and the result is written back to the first one. In the case of 8-bit additions the N flag is reset, P/V is interpreted as overflow. The rest of the flags is modified by definition. On the other hand, 16-bit additions preserve the S, Z and P/V flags, and H is undefined. For example, if the result is out of the range (8 or 16 bits depending on the first operand), the carry flag is set.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

The following 16-bit combinations are valid:
  • add hl, bc|de|hl|sp
  • add ix, bc|de|ix|sp
  • add iy, bc|de|iy|sp

item.name: sub
item.syntax: sub op8
item.description: The value of the operand is subtracted from A, and the result is also written back to A. The N flag is set, P/V is interpreted as overflow. The rest of the flags is modified by definition. There is no 16-bit version.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: adc
item.syntax: adc a,op8 (8 bits) or adc hl,reg16 (16 bits, reg16 can be BC, DE, HL or SP)
item.description: The sum of the two operands plus the carry flag (0 or 1) is calculated, and the result is written back into the first operand. The N flag is reset, P/V is interpreted as overflow. The rest of the flags is modified by definition. In the case of 16-bit addition the H flag is undefined.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: sbc
item.syntax: sbc a,op8 (8 bits) or sbc hl,reg16 (16 bits, reg16 can be BC, DE, HL or SP)
item.description: The sum of the second operand and the carry flag (0 or 1) is subtracted from the first operand, where the final result is written back as well. The N flag is set, P/V is interpreted as overflow. The rest of the flags is modified by definition. In the case of 16-bit subtraction the H flag is undefined.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: inc
item.syntax: inc op8 (immediate values are of course invalid) or inc reg16 (reg16: BC, DE, HL, IX, IY or SP)
item.description: Increments the value of the operand by one. 8-bit increments preserve the C flag, reset N, treat P/V as overflow and modify the others by definition. 16-bit increments do not alter any of the flags.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: dec
item.syntax: dec op8 (immediate values are of course invalid) or dec reg16 (reg16: BC, DE, HL, IX, IY or SP)
item.description: Decrements the value of the operand by one. 8-bit decrements preserve the C flag, set N, treat P/V as overflow and modify the others by definition. 16-bit decrements do not alter any of the flags.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: neg
item.description: This instruction has no operands. When it is executed, the value in A is multiplied by -1 (two's complement). The N flag is set, P/V is interpreted as overflow. The rest of the flags is modified by definition. This instruction is completely equivalent to cpl a followed by inc a (both in execution time and in size).

item.name: daa
item.description: Decimal Adjust Accumulator; no operands are needed. When this instruction is executed, the A register is BCD corrected using the contents of the flags. The exact process is the following: if the least significant four bits of A contain a non-BCD digit (i. e. it is greater than 9) or the H flag is set, then $06 is added to the register. Then the four most significant bits are checked. If this more significant digit also happens to be greater than 9 or the C flag is set, then $60 is added. If this second addition was needed, the C flag is set after execution, otherwise it is reset. The N flag is preserved, P/V is parity and the others are altered by definition.

item.name: or
item.syntax: or op8
item.description: Performs bit-wise OR between A and the operand, and writes the result back to A. The C and N flags are cleared, P/V is parity, and the others are altered by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: and
item.syntax: and op8
item.description: Performs bit-wise AND between A and the operand, and writes the result back to A. The C and N flags are cleared, P/V is parity, and the others are altered by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: xor
item.syntax: xor op8
item.description: Performs bit-wise XOR (exclusive or) between A and the operand, and writes the result back to A. The C and N flags are cleared, P/V is parity, and the others are altered by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: cpl
item.description: This instruction has no operands. It gives the one's complement of A, i.e. all the bits of A are reversed individually. It sets the H and N flags, and leaves the others intact.

item.name: set
item.syntax: set n,op8 (n is a number between 0 and 7, op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: As its name suggests, this instruction sets the n<sup>th</sup> bit of the operand given. The flags are preserved.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: res
item.syntax: res n,op8 (n is a number between 0 and 7, op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: This instruction resets the n<sup>th</sup> bit of the operand given. The flags are preserved.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: scf
item.description: This is the Set Carry Flag instruction, which simply alters the flags. Besides setting the C flag, it clears H and N as well.

item.name: ccf
item.description: Complement Carry Flag, i.e. negating the C flag. Besides, N is cleared and H holds an undefined value after execution.

item.name: sla
item.syntax: sla op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: The bits of the operand are shifted leftwards, bit 0 (the least significant bit) is reset. The bit leaving the operand on the left appears in the carry. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: sra
item.syntax: sra op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: The bits of the operand are shifted rightwards, except for bit 7 (the most significant bit), whose value is left intact (the sign is preserved). The bit leaving the operand on the right appears in the carry. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: sll/sl1
item.syntax: sll op8 or sl1 op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: The bits of the operand are shifted leftwards, bit 0 is set to one. The bit leaving the operand on the left is lost. The C, H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: This instruction is undocumented, and incompatible with the subsequent generations of the CPU (e. g. the Z380 does not support it), therefore you should avoid using it. However, it should work on every Z80 processor.
The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: srl
item.syntax: srl op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: The bits of the operand are shifted rightwards, bit 7 is reset. The bit leaving the operand on the right appears in the carry. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: rl
item.syntax: rl op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: 9-bit rotation to the left. The bit leaving on the left is copied into the carry, while the old value of the carry appears in bit 0 of the operand. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: rr
item.syntax: rr op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: 9-bit rotation to the right. The bit leaving on the right is copied into the carry, while the old value of the carry appears in bit 7 of the operand. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: rla
item.description: There are no operands to this instruction. It is the same as rl a except that it is faster and does not change the S, Z and P/V flags.

item.name: rra
item.description: There are no operands to this instruction. It is the same as rr a except that it is faster and does not change the S, Z and P/V flags.

item.name: rlc
item.syntax: rlc op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: 8-bit rotation to the left. The bit leaving on the left is copied into the carry, and to bit 0 of the operand as well. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: rrc
item.syntax: rrc op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: 8-bit rotation to the right. The bit leaving on the right is copied into the carry, and to bit 7 of the operand as well. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: rlca
item.description: There are no operands to this instruction. It is the same as rlc a except that it is faster and preserves the S, Z and P/V flags.

item.name: rrca
item.description: There are no operands to this instruction. It is the same as rrc a except that it is faster and preserves the S, Z and P/V flags.

item.name: rld
item.description: This instruction has no operands. It is a 4-bit leftward rotation of the 12-bit number whose 4 most significant bits are the 4 least significant bits of A, and its 8 least significant bits are in (HL). I. e. if A contains %aaaaxxxx and (HL) is %yyyyzzzz initially, their final values will be A=%aaaayyyy and (HL)=%zzzzxxxx. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition. The carry flag is preserved.

item.name: rrd
item.description: This instruction has no operands. It is a 4-bit rightward rotation of the 12-bit number whose 4 most significant bits are the 4 least significant bits of A, and its 8 least significant bits are in (HL). I. e. if A contains %aaaaxxxx and (HL) is %yyyyzzzz initially, their final values will be A=%aaaazzzz and (HL)=%xxxxyyyy. The H and N flags are reset, P/V is parity, S and Z are modified by definition. The carry flag is preserved.

item.name: cp
item.syntax: cp op8
item.description: This is a virtual subtraction from A, without writing back the result. You can regard it as a sub op8 that affects only the flags. The most important examples: if A = op8 then the C flag is reset, and Z is set. If A < op8, C is set and Z is reset. If A > op8 then both C and Z are reset.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: bit
item.syntax: bit n,op8 (op8 cannot be immediate, IXH, IXL, IYH or IYL)
item.description: The opposite of the n<sup>th</sup> bit of the second operand is written into the Z flag. The carry is left intact, N is reset, H is set, while S and P/V are undefined.

note.description: The notation op8 denotes either A, B, C, D, E, H, L, IXH, IXL, IYH, IYL, (HL), (IX+n), (IY+n) or an 8-bit immediate (constant).

item.name: cpi
item.description: There are no operands. This is a cp (hl); inc hl; dec bc combined in one instruction. The carry is preserved, N is set and all the other flags are affected as defined. P/V denotes the overflowing of BC, while the Z flag is set if A=(HL) at the time of the comparison.

item.name: cpd
item.description: The same as cpi but with HL being decremented.

item.name: cpir
item.description: Simply cpi repeated until either BC becomes zero or A is equal to (HL).

item.name: cpdr
item.description: The only difference from cpir is that HL is decremented during each iteration.

item.name: jp/jr
item.syntax: jp/jr label or (hl)/(ix)/(iy) (unconditional) or jp/jr condition,label (conditional)
item.description: When arriving at any of these intructions, execution is immediately continued from the location denoted by the label given (you can use numbers instead of labels of course). If the operand is a register reference (e.g. <tt>jp (hl)</tt>), it means that the value of the register will be loaded into PC directly; these jumps can be unconditional only. Otherwise, if the condition is not fulfilled, execution continues as if there wasn't any jump. The flags are preserved in all the cases.

note.description:
The conditions can be the following:
ConditionMeaning
cCarry (C) is set
ncCarry is not set
zZero (Z) is set
nzZero is not set
mSign (S) is set
pSign is not set
peParity/overflow (P/V) is set
poParity/overflow is not set
The most important difference between the two jumps is that jr calculates the jump address with respect to itself, and the destination must be in its 128-byte vicinity. Moreover, jr can only take the first four conditions of those listed above. Besides all these differences, jr is slower but also smaller than the corresponding jp, therefore you have to decide whether speed or size is more important in the piece of code you place the jump into.

item.name: djnz
item.syntax: djnz label
item.description: B is decreased, and a jr label happens if the result was zero. The flags are preserved. Since this is a relative jump, it can only point to its 128-byte vicinity.

item.name: call
item.syntax: call label (unconditional) or call condition,label (conditional)
item.description: The address of the instruction immediately following the call (i. e. PC+3) is saved to the stack, and execution is continued from the address given by the label. The conditions are the same as those of the absolute jump, i.e. all the eight conditions can be used. The flags are preserved.

item.name: ret
item.syntax: ret (unconditional) or ret condition (conditional)
item.description: The word on the top of the stack is retrieved and it is used as the address of the next instruction from which the execution is to be continued. This is basically a <tt>pop pc</tt>. The conditions work the same way as above, all of them can be used. The flags are preserved.

item.name: di
item.description: There are no operands. When di is executed, the (maskable) interrupts are disabled. The flags are preserved.

item.name: ei
item.description: There are no operands. When ei is executed, the (maskable) interrupts are enabled. The flags are preserved.

item.name: reti
item.description: This instruction returns from the interrupt routine. From the programmer's point of view it is no more than a simple ret, but it has to be used to properly handle the interrupt. Note that you cannot use conditions with reti. The flags are not affected.

item.name: retn
item.description: Basically the same as reti, but this one is used to return from the nonmaskable interrupt (NMI).

item.name: im
item.syntax: im mode (mode is 0, 1 or 2)
item.description: Setting the interrupt mode. The flags are not affected. Mode 0 means that only external devices generate interrupts, and the devices send instructions (mostly rst) to the CPU to execute. (this is not used in the TI calculators). When mode 1 is active, interrupts are generated by the internal circuitry of the processor, and each time this occurs, rst $38 is executed. The frequency of these interrupts is 200 per second on a ZX Spectrum, but it depends on the state of the batteries in the case of TI calculators (probably varies between 100 and 150). Mode 2 causes the user interrupt to be executed instead of rst $38.

item.name: rst
item.description: The long name of this instruction is Restart; it is basically a call to the given address. The action taken by the rst's depends on what kind of hardware you have. On the TI calculators they are equivalent to some of the most important ROM calls. Another example from the Spectrum: rst $28 activates the built-in floating point calculator. The rst instruction does not affect the flags either.

item.name: in
item.syntax: in a,(n) (n is a byte) or in reg8,(c) (where reg8 is A, B, C, D, E, H or L)
item.description: Reading from the port marked by the second operand, and storing the result into the first operand. The carry is preserved, N is cleared, P/V is parity and the other flags are affected by definition. There exists an undocumented version as well: <tt>in (c)</tt> (or <tt>in f,(c)</tt> depending on what you compile your programs with), which does not store the result anywhere, but still alters the flags.

item.name: out
item.syntax: out (n),a (n is a byte) or out (c),reg8 (where reg8 is A, B, C, D, E, H or L)
item.description: Writing the value of the second operand to the port given in the first operand. The flags are preserved. Similarly to in there is an undocumented combination: out (c),0 can be used, too.

item.name: ini
item.description: There are no operands. Reads the (C) port and writes the result to (HL), then increments HL and decrements B (not BC!). The carry is preserved, the N flag is reset, while S, H and P/V are undefined. Z is set if B becomes zero after decrementing, otherwise it is reset.

item.name: ind
item.description: The same as ini with the minor difference that HL is decremented.

item.name: inir
item.description: This is ini repeated until B becomes zero. Therefore the Z flag is always set on leaving the instruction.

item.name: indr
item.description: The same as inir, but the memory is naturally filled backwards.

item.name: outi
item.description: There are no operands. Reads the byte at (HL) and outputs it to the (C) port, then increments HL and decrements B. The carry is preserved, the N flag is reset, while S, H and P/V are undefined. Z is set if B becomes zero after decrementing, otherwise it is reset.

item.name: outd
item.description: The same as outi with the minor difference that HL is decremented.

item.name: otir
item.description: This is outi repeated until B becomes zero. Therefore the Z flag is always set on leaving the instruction.

item.name: otdr
item.description: The same as outir, but the memory is naturally read backwards.

item.name: nop
item.description: This instruction waits for an elementary amount of time: 4 clock cycles, and does nothing else.

item.name: halt
item.description: The execution of instructions is suspended and the CPU enters the low power state until an interrupt occurs. Note: when the calculator is switched off, it is actually running a series of <tt>halt</tt>'s while waiting for On to be pressed.
